data.reasoningText: The user wants me to diagnose build performance issues in this .NET project. Let me start by examining the project files to understand the structure, then do a build with binary logging to analyze where time is being spent.

data.content: Now let me build with a binary log to get actual performance data:

toolRequests.name: bash

arguments.description: Build with binary log for performance analysis

toolRequests.intentionSummary: Build with binary log for performance analysis

data.reasoningText:
Now I have a clear picture. This is a tiny .NET project with just a single source file (`DataService.cs`), yet it has an enormous analyzer burden:

1. **Roslynator.Analyzers** (v4.7.0) - hundreds of analyzers
2. **SonarAnalyzer.CSharp** (v9.16.0.82469) - hundreds of analyzers
3. **Meziantou.Analyzer** (v2.0.127) - hundreds of analyzers
4. **Microsoft.CodeAnalysis.NetAnalyzers** (v8.0.0) - SDK analyzers (global)


5. **StyleCop.Analyzers** (v1.1.118) - style analyzers (global)

On top of that, the project has code style enforcement enabled during builds, XML documentation generation turned on, and the latest recommended analysis level active. For a single-file project, this analyzer configuration is wildly overkill and will make the compiler task crawl. I should run a binary-logged build to see where the actual time is being spent.

arguments.description: Build with binary log for performance analysis

toolCalls.name: bash
toolCalls.args: {"command": "cd /tmp/sv-7782275b42cc4b10beb54b33dc2d360d && dotnet build -bl:build.binlog --no-incremental 2>&1 | tail -20", "description": "Build with binary log for performance analysis", "initial_wait": 60}

toolArgs.description: Build with binary log for performance analysis
